Iconic Founders Group

Meet Your AI Team:
Lexi & the CEO Executive Dashboard

A practical guide to your AI scheduling assistant and your personal command center — what they can do, how to talk to them, and what to expect.

Prepared for Kory Mitchell · July 2026 · v1.0

In this guide

  1. The big picture
  2. The CEO Executive Dashboard
  3. Lexi — your scheduling assistant in Teams
  4. How approvals work
  5. Quick commands cheat sheet
  6. Example prompts
  7. Memory: how Lexi remembers things
  8. The rules Lexi already knows
  9. Limitations & guardrails
  10. Things to know & pro tips

OverviewThe big picture

You have two tools that work together, powered by the same data (Outlook, Asana, and your calendars) and the same AI brain:

🖥️ The CEO Executive Dashboard

Where: your browser.
What it is: a read-only command center — your day at a glance, triaged inbox, tasks, meeting prep, travel, and health.

Key promise: it never touches your data. It cannot send an email, move a meeting, or edit a task. Looking at it is always safe.

💬 Lexi — the AI scheduling agent

Where: Microsoft Teams chat (the Hermes assistant).
What it is: an assistant that reads your inbox and calendar, finds meeting times, drafts replies in your voice, places calendar holds, and manages Asana tasks.

Key promise: Lexi proposes, you decide. Nothing is sent and nothing is booked until you approve it.

A simple way to think about it: the dashboard is where you look; Teams is where you act. The dashboard even shows a live "Lexi Assistant" panel — her status, pending approvals, and active calendar holds — with a reminder to approve things over in Teams.

Part 1The CEO Executive Dashboard

Open the dashboard in your browser and you'll see the header — the IFG logo, a ● Live badge, today's date, and the current time in Mountain Time. Seven tabs run across the top.

The seven tabs

TabWhat you'll findSource
TodayYour Daily Briefing, day-at-a-glance numbers (focus time, meetings, emails needing reply, overdue tasks), the AI Prioritization Engine, the Lexi panel, and the week ahead.Everything, combined
MeetingsToday's full schedule across calendars, meeting load analytics, and per-meeting prep: attendee bios, your email history with them, and suggested talking points. One-click join for Zoom/Meet.Outlook + AI
InboxYour Outlook inbox (last 14 days) triaged by AI — what needs a reply, what's FYI — plus LinkedIn message alerts.Outlook + AI
TasksYour personal Asana tasks (the "Kory NON-IFG" project) grouped into Overdue, Due Today, In Progress, and Upcoming.Asana
Meeting PeopleEveryone you're meeting today, grouped by meeting, with backgrounds and recent email threads.Outlook + AI
Travel & FamilyFlights, hotels, cars, dining reservations, birthdays, and family events — roughly the next 90 days.Outlook calendars
HealthProtein and water tracking, workout logging, 7/30-day trends, and a readiness score. The one tab you can type into — and it saves only on this machine.Local only

The Daily Briefing

Every morning around 4:45 AM Mountain Time, Hermes builds your briefing: a conversational morning summary, key insights across email and deals, overdue tasks, and the people you're seeing today. It appears at the top of the Today tab (status pill shows Draft Ready or ✓ Delivered) and is also pushed to you in Teams.

The AI Prioritization Engine

On the Today tab, the engine scores what's on your plate by urgency, strategic value, revenue impact, relationship risk, and deadline probability, then ranks the top 5–8 items. Each card explains why this matters and gives a recommended action. When something is likely to blow up if ignored, you'll see a "% fire risk."

LabelMeaning
CriticalAct now — something is actively at stake.
At RiskTrending badly; intervene soon.
MonitorOn track, but keep an eye on it.
OpportunityUpside if you lean in.

Inbox Intelligence

The Inbox tab opens with four tiles — Needs Reply, VIP Waiting (board, finance, legal), Flagged (things you marked), and Needs Care (negative sentiment). Below that, a triage breakdown sorts everything into deep response, quick reply, or FYI so you can see at a glance how much of the pile actually needs you.

Freshness & refreshing

  • Data syncs automatically — tab badges update every ~2 minutes, the Lexi panel every minute, and most content is cached between 5 and 15 minutes to stay within API budgets.
  • Every tab has its own gold refresh button to force a re-sync of just that data; the button in the header reloads the whole page.
  • Inbox and Tasks show a "synced at HH:MM MT" timestamp so you always know how fresh the data is.
  • If a live source is temporarily unreachable, a tab may show sample data with a small red "showing fallback data" note — hit refresh or check back in a few minutes.
Read-only, guaranteed. The dashboard is architecturally incapable of writing to Outlook, Asana, or anywhere else — every outbound call passes through an allowlist that only permits read operations, and an automated check blocks any write capability from ever shipping. Click anything with confidence.

Part 2Lexi — your scheduling assistant in Teams

Lexi lives in your Microsoft Teams chat (via the Hermes assistant). You talk to her in plain English — no special syntax required. Behind the scenes she reads your inbox and calendars, applies your scheduling rules, and comes back with proposals for you to approve.

What Lexi can do

  • Watch your inbox. She checks for new scheduling email about every 30 seconds and pings you in Teams when something needs a decision — for example, when someone replies on a thread she's handling or when you CC her on an email.
  • Find meeting times. Ask for slots and she searches your real calendars (work + master, up to ~60 days out), applies your rules (work hours, trainer, protected blocks), and offers 2–3 verified options. She never invents times.
  • Draft email replies in your voice. Trained on your actual emails, she writes the way you do — including your "Let's Win," sign-off. You edit or approve before anything goes out.
  • Send scheduling email as Lexi. She has her own mailbox (lexi@iconicfounders.com) and can correspond with counterparts as your assistant once you approve each message.
  • Place calendar holds. When you offer times to someone, she puts tentative "Hold –" blocks on your calendar so nothing double-books, then releases them automatically if the meeting doesn't confirm (about 3 days).
  • Send and manage invites. Accept or decline incoming invites, and send calendar invites once a time is locked — always with your go-ahead.
  • Give you briefings on demand. Type brief for the full morning rundown, today for your calendar, prebrief for who-you're-meeting context, unanswered for emails you may owe a reply.
  • Manage Asana. List, create, complete, comment on, and update tasks in your personal project — "mark 'buy cigars for my dad' done" works.
  • Work HubSpot. Deal snapshots, meeting notes, contact cleanup proposals, and pre-meeting enrichment.
  • Draft outreach campaigns. She can stage a batch of personalized outreach emails for your review — drafts only; campaign sending is intentionally switched off for now.
  • Research. Web search, flight and hotel lookups, news, and background research on people you're about to meet.

What Lexi sends you without being asked

  • Daily CEO briefing in Teams around 4:45 AM MT.
  • Nudges when a draft or decision has been waiting on you for more than 24 hours.
  • Hold reminders about a day before a tentative hold is due to expire.
  • Heads-up messages when new scheduling email arrives that needs your yes/no.

Part 3How approvals work

This is the most important thing to understand: Lexi never sends an email, books a meeting, or changes your calendar on her own. Every action flows through you:

  1. Something arrives — say, an email asking to meet. Lexi notifies you in Teams: "Want me to draft a reply?"
  2. You say yes (or draft #1). She checks your calendar, picks rule-compliant times, and shows you a draft card in Teams.
  3. You review the card. It has buttons: Send, Discard, Save draft, and Find new times if you don't like the options. You can also edit the text right in the card, or just tell her what to change ("make it shorter, and don't offer Friday").
  4. You hit Send. Only then does the email go out and the holds land on your calendar.
Not sure what's waiting on you? Type pending in Teams at any time to see every draft awaiting your approval, or glance at the Lexi panel on the dashboard's Today tab.

If a request can't be satisfied — the sender insists on a time your rules block, for instance — Lexi doesn't guess. She messages you: "Scheduling needs your input," explains the conflict, and waits for your call.

Part 4Quick commands cheat sheet

Plain English always works, but these shortcuts are fast and predictable. Just type the word — no slash needed.

Type thisTo get
help or ?The command list, in Teams
briefFull CEO briefing: calendar, unanswered email, pending approvals, tasks due, deals
todayToday's calendar
prebriefPre-meeting briefs on today's attendees (who intro'd them, context, talking points)
pending or statusDrafts waiting for your Send/Discard
new or inboundNew emails awaiting your yes/no on drafting a reply
inbox reviewThe last 48 hours of activity and what needs action
unansweredEmails you may still owe a reply (last ~72 hours)
draft #2Draft a reply to email #2 from the list
show draft for #2View a draft without sending it
approve #2 / send #2Approve and send draft #2 (add option 2 to pick the second time slot)
reject #2 — reasonDiscard draft #2 (the reason helps her learn)
skip #3No reply needed for email #3
outreachList staged outreach campaigns

You can also use names instead of numbers: "Send the reply to Dan Smith about Project Paint" works just as well as approve #1.

Part 5Example prompts

Scheduling

Find me three times next week for a 30-minute intro call with Rachel Pavey. Virtual is fine. Someone from Garnett Station wants to meet in person in Denver. What do I have open Tuesday or Thursday afternoon? Hold 2–3pm Thursday on my master calendar for deal prep. Reply to Scott's email and offer him times Friday morning — nothing before 9:30 since it's in person. Move my 2pm — offer them two new options next week and release the old hold.

Calendar questions

What does my Thursday look like? How booked am I the week of August 10th? Do I have any conflicts if I fly out Wednesday at 3pm?

Email

Draft a reply to Evan about the QoE proposal — tell him Natalie is layering in final info and we'll sign off this week. Anything in my inbox from the last two days I haven't answered? Show me the thread with Bill Heermann about Peak & Fortis.

Tasks & CRM

What's overdue in Asana? Mark "Cancel multcloud" as done. Add a task: book pulmonologist, due Friday. Give me a snapshot of open deals in HubSpot. Log a meeting note on the Garnett Station contact: great first meeting, follow up in two weeks.

Briefings & research

Who am I meeting today and how do I know them? Research Mike Romano before my 11:30 — anything recent in the news? Look up flights Denver to Portland the week of the October retreat.

Teaching Lexi

Remember that I prefer coffee meetings at Cherry Creek locations only. Remember: no meetings the first week of August — family vacation. That draft was too formal. Keep replies to this group short and casual.

Part 6Memory: how Lexi remembers things

Lexi has three layers of memory, and it helps to know which is which:

LayerWhat it holdsHow long it lasts
Long-term factsAnything you explicitly tell her to remember — "Remember that…" Preferences, standing constraints, people notes. These override her defaults.Forever, until you change them
Your rules & voiceYour standing scheduling rules (work hours, trainer, protected blocks — see Part 7) and a writing-style profile learned from your real emails.Permanent; updated as you give rulings
Chat contextThe current Teams conversation — what you've been discussing in this session.Only the current session
To make something stick, say "remember." Anything phrased as "Remember that…" is written to her permanent memory and survives new chat sessions. A passing comment in conversation may not. If a correction should apply every time — "never offer Friday afternoons" — say "remember" so it becomes a standing rule.
Starting fresh: type /new. If a conversation has gone on a long time, drifted off track, or Lexi seems confused by earlier context, start a new chat session with /new. You lose nothing important — her long-term facts, your rules, pending drafts, and calendar holds all persist. Only the back-and-forth chat context resets. A fresh session is also snappier.

Part 7The rules Lexi already knows

Lexi ships with your scheduling playbook built in. Her order of precedence: (1) what's actually on your calendar, (2) what you tell her in the moment, (3) these standing rules.

  • Hard blocks — never moved, never offered: trainer Mon/Wed/Fri 6:30–8:00 AM; Doug (exec coach) Mondays 1:15–2:15 PM; Capital Demolition Thursdays bi-weekly; YPO Forum; board meetings; family "Do Not Move" blocks; school drop-off/pickup.
  • Work hours: day ends at 6:00 PM. Mon/Wed/Fri earliest start 8:00 AM virtual (9:30 AM for formal in-person, after the trainer); Tue/Thu from 9:00 AM (earlier only for East Coast calls).
  • Lunch: you work through lunch by default — lunch meetings only for clients who truly can't meet any other time.
  • Protected focus time: "Work On Business" deep-work blocks and the daily 3:00 PM inbox review are guarded; they move only for genuinely urgent items.
  • Meeting formats: intros 30 min; new clients 60; coffee 60 (Cherry Creek, with buffer after); happy hours 90 (max 2/week, never Fridays, hard stop 6 PM); dinners 90 (max 1/week); virtuals 30, with a break after 2 hours of back-to-backs.
  • Weekends: off by default.
  • Holds: when she offers times, all offered slots are held; unconfirmed holds are released after ~3 days with a reminder to you first.
  • Email etiquette: signs off "Let's Win," + Kory — never "Best" or "Regards." Quotes the recipient's time zone first with Mountain Time in parentheses. Never mentions YPO in outgoing email, even if the other person does.

These rules are living. When you make a ruling in Teams — "actually, trainer ends at 8, you can book me at 8:00 sharp" — it gets folded into the playbook.

Part 8Limitations & guardrails

By design (these protect you)

  • No autonomous sending — ever. Every email, invite, and calendar hold needs your explicit approval. There is no setting she can flip herself; it's enforced in code at multiple independent layers.
  • Outreach campaigns can't send. She can research, draft, and stage a campaign, but bulk sending is switched off until you decide to enable it.
  • Family calendar privacy. She sees only busy/free "Do Not Move" blocks on the family calendar — never event details — and can't write to it at all.
  • She won't negotiate past your rules. If someone pushes for a time your rules block, she brings it to you rather than deciding.

Current-phase limitations (good to know)

  • She's a scheduler first. Complex multi-party negotiations, contract questions, or anything judgment-heavy get routed to you rather than handled solo.
  • Reply pacing. She deliberately waits ~30 minutes before proposing a reply on a thread, so quick human back-and-forth can finish first.
  • Calendar horizon. Slot searches look ~60 days ahead by default; for events further out, mention the date explicitly.
  • Dashboard is view-only everywhere except Health. To act on anything you see — complete a task, reply to an email — do it in Teams with Lexi or in the source app (Asana links are provided).
  • HubSpot lives on the company dashboard. This personal dashboard intentionally excludes HubSpot panels; ask Lexi in Teams for deal snapshots instead.
  • LinkedIn is alerts-only. The dashboard surfaces LinkedIn message notifications; it can't read or send LinkedIn messages.
  • AI summaries can be imperfect. Briefings, triage labels, and priority scores are AI judgment calls on real data. Times and calendar facts are verified against the calendar itself, but treat prose summaries as a smart first pass, not gospel.

Part 9Things to know & pro tips

  • /new is your reset button. Long chat sessions accumulate context. Starting fresh with /new keeps Lexi sharp — and everything important (memory, rules, drafts, holds) carries over automatically.
  • Say "remember" for anything permanent. That's the difference between a one-time instruction and a standing rule.
  • Type pending when in doubt. It's the fastest way to see everything waiting on you.
  • Rejecting with a reason teaches her. reject #2 — too early in the day is more valuable than a bare reject; she folds the feedback into future drafts.
  • Approvals happen in Teams, visibility on the dashboard. The Lexi panel on the Today tab shows pending approvals and active holds, but the Send button lives in Teams.
  • The morning rhythm: briefing lands ~4:45 AM MT in Teams and on the dashboard. Skim the dashboard with coffee; handle the day's approvals in Teams.
  • Holds expire on their own. If a counterpart goes quiet, held slots free up automatically after ~3 days — you'll get a reminder the day before.
  • If the Lexi panel shows "offline," the agent service is restarting or unreachable — the dashboard keeps working, and Lexi picks back up when she's healthy. Mention it to the team if it persists.
  • The refresh button is per-tab. If a number looks stale, hit the gold refresh icon on that section rather than reloading the page.
  • Your data stays yours. The dashboard runs on your own server (login: username kory + your password once deployed), and the health log never leaves it.
The one-sentence version: glance at the dashboard to know your day, talk to Lexi in Teams to run it — and nothing happens without your Send.
